content:
The Next Bottleneck: Adoption

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Since the start of enterprise software, one frequent topic of conversation in institutional asset management has been about adoption. Once we’ve built a thing–can we get people to use it? How do we ensure clients are using the latest features, and how can we prevent the dreaded “like for like” upgrades. That conversation is about to get harder. Not because adoption is stalling, but because first: the thing firms are trying to adopt won’t sit still long enough to be adopted well, and second: the “build it yourself” argument looks so much seductively cheaper now.

### The Feature Velocity Problem

AI-driven development tools are fundamentally changing how fast software vendors can ship. Not incrementally faster—categorically faster. Features that used to take a quarter now take weeks. The release notes that used to arrive with your quarterly or annual upgrade now arrive monthly, sometimes weekly. And it’s not just one…every vendor in your stack is experiencing this acceleration simultaneously, or will be shortly if they want to remain in business.

That’s the part nobody’s planning for adequately.

The 2025 DORA report found that 90% of organizations now use AI in their software development workflows, with over 80% reporting measurable productivity increases.1 AlixPartners reports that AI-accelerated coding tools are delivering 20–30% productivity gains in development—but most organizations are failing to capture strategic business value from that speed.2 The velocity is real. What firms do with it on the receiving end is where it breaks down.

Your risk system ships new analytics capabilities. Your portfolio management platform adds an agentic workflow layer. Your data vendor restructures its API. Your reporting tool redesigns its dashboard engine. Your compliance platform integrates a new regulatory feed. All of this lands in the same quarter. Each release requires someone on your team to learn about it, evaluate whether it’s relevant, test it against your environment, build it into your processes, and train the people who need to use it. That’s not a technology problem. That’s a throughput problem, and it’s one that no vendor is going to solve for you because they’re the ones creating it.

The weekend app that broke the AI resistance

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

In the last edition, I promised to share the messy reality of becoming an AI-first software agency. I told you we’re testing everything (well, almost everything) on ourselves before selling it to our clients. The first challenge was to overcome the resistance towards the AI. Here’s how we did it.

---

At first, our engineering team was highly skeptical towards AI. You would think that techies, especially senior engineers, would be the first to adopt AI coding assistants. But Nenad, my co-founder and the tech brain of Holycode, saw the exact opposite happen.

There was a real blockage. Our developers didn’t trust the AI to write good code. They didn’t know how to verify its output efficiently. They were being pushed out of their comfort zone. Developers like writing their own code. They like being in the driver’s seat. Giving up that control felt like giving up their craft, and faith in the tools was low.

So, nobody was really rushing to change how they worked. Until a weekend experiment changed the conversation.

### The wake-up call

I am not a software developer. I’ve been in tech for almost two decades, but I have never written more than one line of code in my life. But the hype around AI coding assistants like Base44 and Lovable was getting too loud to ignore, so in May 2025 I decided to play around.

Over the course of a single weekend (my kids had left, so I had time), using Lovable, I built a fully functional absence management app from scratch.

On Monday morning, I showed it to our team saying I let someone build it for us. I asked them to guess how long it took to build and the range was somewhere around 2-3 months. Then I opened up about the fact it was me, on a weekend, that built it. You could see it was the massive wake-up call I hoped it would be. The general sentiment in the room was palpable: “Damn, if our dumb, non-techie CEO is able to build this by himself, something massive is happening.”

That was the trigger. The engineers realized they couldn’t ignore this anymore. It was time to properly engage.

So in order to emphasize the trigger, we doubled down and said that anyone not actively using AI by the time the next performance review (approx. 4 months away) comes around would be let go.

My Digital Kaizen Stack One Year Later: What Actually Happened When I Built a Personal AI Memory System

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

TL;DR: After 22 years as a financial advisor, I built a personal AI memory system to capture knowledge from thousands of client conversations. The result: 15 pounds lost, a book published, stronger client growth, and faster decision-making across every area of life. Here's how I did it and what you need to know to build your own.

Core Questions Answered:

- Why most AI usage doesn't accumulate value over time
- How to build a capture system for business and personal knowledge
- The weekly reflection ritual behind continuous improvement
- Real outcomes from six months of implementation
- Five practical steps to start your own intelligence system

A year ago I started experimenting with something I called Digital Kaizen. The idea was simple: apply continuous improvement principles to how I capture, organize, and use knowledge in my financial advisory practice.

What I didn't expect was how completely this would change everything. Not how I work. How I notice patterns in health, business growth, and decision-making across my life.

After 22 years of advising state employees and retirees, conducting thousands of client conversations, I had a problem I didn't know existed.

A massive amount of insight was disappearing.

Not the basic facts. Those go into the CRM fine. Account balances, beneficiaries, retirement dates. The systems we use are good at storing structured data.

What they're terrible at: capturing context and patterns across hundreds or thousands of conversations.

I might have five different clients in a month worried about the same retirement issue. Confusion about TRS pension options. Anxiety about required minimum distributions. Concerns about healthcare after leaving a university job.

In the moment, I'm responding to each person individually.

But the larger pattern, what my clients as a group were thinking about, was mostly invisible.

The real asset of a long career, pattern recognition built from thousands of conversations, was going uncaptured.

This is what happened when I decided to build a personal AI intelligence system with memory.

The Software That "Does Everything" Is Costing You More Than You Think

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

C-Mimmi-O is where The Bridger, your vibecoding fractional CMO, lives.

You paid for the enterprise plan. You sat through the onboarding. You migrated your data. You trained your team. Six months later, your people are keeping a parallel spreadsheet because the system still does not do the one thing you actually needed.

Sound familiar?

This is the enterprise software trap. And it is far more common than vendors want you to know.

### The Numbers That Should Make You Uncomfortable

Large software implementations have a reliability problem. A landmark McKinsey study found that large IT projects run an average of 45% over budget and 7% over schedule, while delivering 56% less value than predicted. That is not an outlier. That is the average.

The Standish Group's CHAOS Report has tracked software project outcomes for decades. Their consistent finding: roughly two-thirds of all software projects either fail outright or are significantly challenged. Late, over budget, or missing critical features. Pick one, pick all three.

And here is the part no one puts in the sales deck: most software features never get used. Research by Standish Group found that approximately 64% of software features are rarely or never used by the people who paid for them. You are not buying a tool. You are buying a catalogue, using two pages, and storing the rest in a drawer.

For SMEs, the situation is sharper still. A Gartner survey found that 55% of small businesses reported that purchased software did not fully meet their needs, with complexity and irrelevant features cited as the top reasons. Meanwhile, implementation costs for mid-market ERP or CRM systems routinely run two to five times the annual licence fee once you account for consulting, customisation, integration, and training.

You are paying for someone else's vision of your business.

### Why "One Size Fits All" Fits No One Particularly Well

Enterprise software is built to win procurement decisions, not to solve operational problems. The feature list needs to be long enough to beat competitors in a demo. The interface needs to be recognisable enough to reassure a risk-averse buyer. Confessions of a Compliance Officer: What Your OSJ Wishes More Advisors Knew About Your Messaging and Growing Your Firm 

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

` `` `` `` `` `` `` `

 

 

 

` `` `` `` `` `` `` ` 

 

 ` `` `` `` `

 

 

 

 

What do you do when a senior-level Compliance officer starts confessing? You take notes! 

 

I interviewed a top Compliance officer with one of the largest broker-dealers in North America. Their firm has well over $100 billion in AUM and several thousand advisors across North America. This officer, who will remain anonymous, personally oversees 2,200+ advisory offices across the East Coast and Midwest. 

 

The origin story? I'm helping an advisor with this officer's firm create more compelling messaging about the advisor's work. This was a planned conversation, and even though we started with the original advisor, the conversation quickly expanded to discussing other advisors in need of my expertise. 

 

More importantly, the officer started sharing what they've seen top advisors do that separates them from the average advisor. 

 

Here are the top five takeaways from our conversation... 

 

### 1. Every interaction with Compliance is about perspective, not problems. 

 

This officer told me they wished every advisor who connected with them about any regulator or Compliance-specific incident would see that moment as a learning opportunity. It’s frustrating when advisors or support staff simply see a problem with Compliance as a necessary evil or an inconvenience. 

 

This Compliance officer shared that it’s often not the advisor or support staff’s fault when a problem arises. Maybe they were never told about a Compliance reg. Maybe they misunderstood what was needed from their Compliance department. Or, maybe the expectation was vague or incomplete. 

 

In this officer's words, every conversation or meeting with your Compliance rep is an opportunity to ask questions, get their outside perspective, and mine their experience from what they see happening in other advisory businesses. If anything, your Compliance rep can provide a perspective that can help guide your business to avoid problems and get ahead of the learning curve. 

 

### 2. Top advisors invest in their teams. 

 

I asked what characteristics, habits, and qualities this Compliance Officer sees in top advisors that help those advisors stand out

Compliance deadline (Reg S-P: June 3, 2026 + 72-hour vendor notice) June 3, 2026 is not “a privacy date” — it’s an incident-response operational deadline for smaller RIAs. The updated Regulation S-P expectations include a written incident response program, customer notice as soon as practical (and no later than 30 days), and a 72-hour service-provider notification requirement. That changes the exam conversation from “Do you have policies?” to “Can you execute the timeline with real vendors, real data, and real evidence?” If your vendors can’t notify you fast, you can’t notify clients fast. What are you doing to prepare?#RegSP#SEC#RIA#CyberRisk#AdvisorDefense

2w

A compliance manager once told me they spent weeks chasing a vendor for their SOC 2 report. When it finally came through, it was a SOC 3. The vendor thought they were being helpful. The compliance team had to start over. Here's the thing: SOC 1, SOC 2, and SOC 3 sound similar, but they serve completely different purposes. And if you don't specify exactly what you need, you might get something that looks official but doesn't actually meet your due diligence requirements. SOC 1 is about financial reporting. SOC 2 is about data protection. SOC 3 is the CliffsNotes version that's great for a vendor's website but not much else. And within SOC 1 and SOC 2, you've got Type I (snapshot in time) and Type II (tested over months). For most institutions, Type II is the only one worth relying on. We put together a visual guide that breaks it all down so your team knows exactly what to request and why: https://hubs.ly/Q04c2pdF0#VendorManagement#ThirdPartyRisk#SOCReports#BankCompliance

Compliance and marketing have never operated on the same timeline inside a financial institution. In a stable rate environment, that friction was manageable. In the environment banks are navigating now, it is not. This edition of Naehas Navigator is about what the institutions that resolved it did first. 

 

### $175 Billion in Rate Cuts 

 

The Federal Reserve cut rates by a combined 175 basis points across 2024 and 2025. Banks entered 2026 facing headwinds in net interest income while competition for deposits remains intense, particularly among regional institutions. Consumers are more attentive to rate than they have been in years. The window to act is real, and it is compressed. 

 

Most institutions have the strategy. Few have the infrastructure to execute it at the speed the market now requires. Product launches still move through manual review cycles. Disclosures still require weeks of coordination. A decision gets made in a boardroom and stalls somewhere between legal, marketing, and product before it ever reaches a customer. 

 

The institutions differentiating themselves right now are not doing so on rate alone. They are the ones where a product decision made on Monday reaches a customer on Thursday. 

 

---

 

### A $215 Billion Bank Evaluated Every Major Vendor 

 

A $215 billion institution evaluated every major platform in this space before making a selection. The criteria reflected something most RFPs fail to surface: whether a platform could support the actual workflow at scale, without making engineering a dependency for routine changes. 

 

The institution had been repeatedly rebuilding the same base products. Every pricing modification and disclosure update required a separate development project. The business had a strategy. The infrastructure could not keep pace with it. 

 

Four things determined the outcome: integrated product, pricing, and disclosure management in a single environment; customization demonstrated live rather than promised on a roadmap; governance controls that preserved compliance without adding friction; and an API-first architecture aligned with how modern institutions operate.

# Measuring Compliance Risk in Advisory Practices

Founder, Chambers Compliance Advisory Group | I help financial advisors identify their compliance risks and fix them properly

1mo

Every advisory practice has compliance risk. Most advisors just don’t measure it. Compliance risk is not binary. It’s not whether you’re compliant or you’re not. Compliance risk exists on a spectrum. Every practice sits somewhere along it. If I ask you if you believe you’re compliant, you might answer with some version of: • “Of course.” • “I’ve been doing this for 20 years.” • “I’ve never had an issue.” • “I passed my last audit.” • “My MGA hasn’t raised any concerns.” But that’s not measurement. That’s just an assumption. Without measurement, you can’t distinguish between: • low risk versus moderate risk versus high risk • isolated gaps versus systemic weaknesses • operational inconsistency versus structural exposure Once you start measuring, you might find things you didn’t expect. And once you know something, you can’t unknow it. The issue isn’t whether risk exists in your practice. It does. The issue is whether you can see it. If you’re relying on just assumption: • you overestimate how strong your practice is • you underestimate how exposed you are • you delay fixing what actually matters So stop thinking in terms of whether you’re “compliant” or not. Instead, ask yourself where your practice is exposed and how you know. That’s when compliance stops being a guessing game. And starts becoming something you can actually control.

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

3w

Through recent guidance and enforcement actions, the SEC has made clear that so-called ‘hedge clauses' – provisions that limit an adviser's liability to gross negligence or willful misconduct, or that suggest clients waive certain legal rights – may mislead clients and conflict with an adviser's fiduciary duty. Cases against advisory firms have found that common formulations – such as limiting liability to gross negligence, disclaiming responsibility for good-faith decisions, or requiring clients to indemnify the adviser – can violate antifraud provisions. https://kitc.es/41fds8n Isaac Mamaysky, Partner of Potomac Law (PLG) and Cofounder and COO of QuantStreet Capital, explains how to identify hedge clauses, why hedge clauses have become such a significant regulatory concern, and how advisers can revise their IMAs without raising compliance red flags.

Why The SEC May No Longer Allow “Hedge Clauses” In Client Advisory Agreements (And How To Replace Them Compliantly) https://www.kitces.com

Limiting liability in the contract doesn’t reduce fiduciary responsibility, it just increases the risk that regulators see it as misleading. Michael Kitces

Investment Compliance: A Fiduciary’s Guide to Staying Ahead

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Compliance is often seen as paperwork or a box to check. But for fiduciaries, it’s a frontline defense for clients and for you.

Staying ahead means embedding compliance into your investment process, not treating it as an afterthought.

Key Compliance Areas

1. Regulatory Requirements Know the laws and standards that apply to your fiduciary role. These vary by jurisdiction but generally emphasize prudence, loyalty, and documentation.

2. Investment Policy Adherence Ensure investments and decisions align with the IPS and documented client objectives.

3. Conflict of Interest Management Disclose any potential conflicts and manage them transparently.

4. Record Keeping Maintain detailed and organized documentation of decisions, communications, and reviews.

5. Regular Training and Updates Stay informed on regulatory changes, new rulings, and best practices.

Embedding Compliance Into Culture

Compliance works best when it’s part of everyday practice, not just annual reviews.

A strong compliance framework protects your clients, your reputation, and your peace of mind.

It’s more than rules. It’s part of what makes fiduciary work meaningful and trusted.

If you’re an RIA hoping 2026 will be a “lighter” year for cybersecurity… the SEC just told us otherwise. | Philip Coniglio

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

# Philip Coniglio’s Post

6mo

If you’re an RIA hoping 2026 will be a “lighter” year for cybersecurity… the SEC just told us otherwise. The Division of Examinations’ 2026 priorities double-down on information security, operational resiliency, and the new reality of AI-driven attacks. Not just for the big firms, but for advisers of all sizes. A few takeaways I think every RIA leadership team should internalize: 1️⃣ Cyber is a perennial exam priority, not a side topic. Examiners will be looking at governance, data loss prevention, access controls, account management, and how you respond and recover from cyber incidents, especially ransomware. 2️⃣ Identity theft and account takeover are front and center. Under Regulation S-ID, they’ll assess whether your written Identity Theft Prevention Program actually detects red flags and includes training, not just a policy on the shelf. 3️⃣ Reg S-P amendments are now “real.” Examiners will ask how you’re building incident response programs that detect, respond to, and recover from unauthorized access to customer information. Most importantly, how you’ll notify affected clients in a timely, risk-based way. 4️⃣ AI is no longer a buzzword, it’s a control topic. The SEC will review whether your use of AI (for surveillance, AML, trading, back-office, etc.) matches your disclosures and is supervised with real policies and procedures. What does this mean for RIAs practically? Your WISP, Cyber Policy, and IRP need to line up with Reg S-P and Reg S-ID down to roles, playbooks, and 72-hour style notification triggers. You should be able to show a risk assessment, a living risk register, and evidence of training exercises that include ransomware, account takeover, and vendor incidents. If you’re experimenting with AI tools, you need guardrails: data handling, human oversight, and a paper trail that proves you’re not letting automation drive unsupervised decisions that affect clients. At AdvisorDefense, this is exactly where we’re helping RIAs right now: turning these “priorities” into concrete controls, evidence, and playbooks that will stand up in an exam and in a real incident. If you are contemplating establishing your own Registered Investment Adviser (RIA), it's crucial to address potential pain-points related to compliance and back-office tasks. Hiring a compliance consultant is essential for drafting regulatory documents like ADV, 2B, Privacy Policy, and firm Policies and Procedures, required for filing with state or federal security regulators. 

 

 

 

Transitioning from state to federal registration entails rewriting the ADV and navigating changes in testing and filing requirements. Exploring alternatives, joining a larger RIA can alleviate compliance officer responsibilities, particularly if it offers improved technology and productivity. 

 

 

 

Hiring a compliance consultant will be necessary to write up regulatory documents you will need such as the firm ADV, 2B, Privacy Policy and firm Policy and Procedures policies that need to be written and filed with either state or federal security regulators. 

 

 

 

Taking on a Chief Compliance Officer (CCO) role brings on many responsibilities. You should be aware compliance consultants do not typically take on the Chief Compliance role which ultimately makes you responsible to creating, testing and comply with the security regulations. You should be prepared for a regulatory firm audit within a year of setting up your RIA. During an audit, you will be required to complete all the requests of the regulators, and this can be time-consuming and take away your time with clients. The costs for a compliance consultant will vary on how much consulting time they take on with your firm. 

 

Should your firm be transitioning from State to Federal registration with the SEC, there are numerous changes that will require you to rewrite your ADV. Firm testing requirement will vary with from state firms as will filing requirements with outside state registrations. It can make sense to evaluate other options and consider joining a larger RIA that will elevate you of the compliance officer responsibilities especially if you can improve your tech stake, and productivity.

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

Sam G. Huszczo, CFP, CFA’s firm pulled in 57% of new clients from social media last year. The average RIA? About 10%. He started six years ago. “It’s not like you do three posts and get results next week,” Huszczo told InvestmentNews after winning Advisor of the Year for the Midwest. “It took me six years to get to a point where it’s an actual legitimate lead-gen.” Six years of showing up consistently. Six years of posting content people actually wanted to read. Six years before social media became his primary growth engine. Most advisors quit after six months. They post a few times, get crickets, and decide social media doesn’t work for wealth management. Huszczo’s firm now manages $570 million. He’s got 35,000 Instagram followers and 19,000 on LinkedIn. None of that happened quickly. Three months into building your social presence and wondering why it’s not working yet? That’s normal. The firms winning on social media in 2026 started in 2020. The ones that will win in 2032 are starting now. Link to the full InvestmentNews story in the comments.

This story really shows that genuine relationship-building online is a marathon, not a sprint. Consistency over years is the real differentiator, not just posting frequency. It’s about providing value that resonates, building trust brick by brick. ✨

How I Learned to Get Consulting Leads

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

(First published on my customer acquisition blog.)

I love consulting. I make a good living, I’m rewarded by seeing companies succeed—I’ve seen clients go through two acquisition exits, multiple million-dollar funding rounds, 10x growth in revenue, and more—and I get enough inquiries through this blog and referrals that I don’t face pipeline droughts.

But getting to this point wasn’t easy. In fact, it took a year. The journey taught me a lesson about customer acquisition that I’ve since applied for myself and my clients. What follows is the short but unfiltered story.

## Month 0

There was a long winter after I quit my job. Nevermind that it was summer—what followed was three months in which my savings depleted like the fat of a hibernating animal. I was to become an independent consultant.

I spent those three months doing what I thought one does to find clients: network. I went to meetups every week and gave my elevator pitch to anyone who’d listen. It didn’t occur to me at the time that everyone else was there for the same reason. I was pitching marketing services to a lawyer who was pitching legal services to an MBA “ideas person” who was there to pitch ideas to a… It went on and on, and it was ridiculous.

## Month 3

After three months, my savings were running low and I dreadfully considered going back to a normal job.

Then, a friend I made at one of these meetups referred me to a startup that could use my help. A few calls and a meeting later, we had a deal.

It didn’t lead to other clients, but I got two important things from that project: cash and confidence. It was enough money for another three months of runway.

Maybe that was the formula, I thought; meet smart people and take time to form relationships. I spent the next two months taking that approach and it failed, too. The issue is painfully obvious in hindsight: founders don’t have time to schmooze at random meetups.

## Month 6

Major progress doesn’t happen gradually, it happens in sudden leaps.

The next leap came from an inconspicuous comment I made on Hacker News, which led to an email inquiry, which turned into a project, which turned into a long-term consulting relationship. (They’re still a client today, two years later.)

# Wealth Channel Fundraising: The Scaling Problem

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

Rohan Marwaha

3w

- Report this post

Here's the little known secret about wealth channel fundraising for alternative asset managers: Most funds don’t struggle with raising from HNWIs because demand isn’t there. They struggle because they rapidly hit The Scaling Problem. It shows up the same way, every time: A fund finally gets momentum when raising from advisors…allocations start coming in…and suddenly the bottleneck isn’t sales. It’s everything that happens after “yes.” We’ve seen Investor Services / Ops teams get pulled into a spiral: 📌 more subscriptions → more exceptions 📌 more advisors → more “one-off” platform requirements 📌 more volume → more follow-ups, status checks, document chasing 📌 more investors → more servicing, reporting, tax doc coordination Why? Fundraising from HNWIs is a fundamentally different operational task than raising from institutional LPs. And the process you perfected for nailing fundraising ops? It simply doesn't work in the wealth channel. Most firms try to “solve” the Scaling Problem in the most obvious way: hire. So the Investor Services team balloons… just to keep up with rote operational work. That’s not scaling. That’s staffing. The contrarian take: wealth-channel distribution isn’t primarily a relationship game anymore. It’s a throughput game. If your ops stack can’t handle volume with consistency, your fundraising ceiling shows up fast: ☑️ cycle times stretch ☑️ error rates rise ☑️ the advisor experience degrades ☑️ platforms lose confidence ☑️ your team spends its week answering “where are we on this?” instead of moving capital The punchline: the funds that win won’t just “expand distribution.” They’ll eliminate friction with better rails—standardization, automation, and real workflow infrastructure. What’s the most painful part of “The Scaling Problem” you’ve seen in practice Subscriptions? Suitability? Support? Servicing/reporting? Why High Net Worth Families Are Ignoring Your Content and What To Do About It

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

There is a growing disconnect in wealth management today. Financial advisors serving high net worth families are putting in the work, managing hundreds of millions in assets, building diverse teams, and delivering deep generational value. But their digital presence tells none of that story.

I recently spoke with a managing director at a major Wall Street firm. His team oversees over 600 million in assets and is recognized for its cultural diversity and strong client relationships. His vision is clear. Build a billion dollar practice that helps families preserve and grow wealth across generations.

Despite all that credibility, his brand online feels generic.

Most content distributed from corporate marketing teams is safe but forgettable. It meets compliance standards but does not connect with real people.

This is the hidden risk many advisors face. Your ideal clients, especially those with one to twenty million in investable assets, are looking you up long before they ever schedule a meeting. If your digital presence does not speak to them, someone else's will.

Personalized storytelling is no longer a luxury. It is a strategic necessity.

## Recommended by LinkedIn

The Sunday Summary: Repeat business, quick notes… Mickey Mellen 2 months ago

Why We Love Stories (And Why You Should Start Telling… Melanie Glace 2 years ago

The Year in Review Nandini Das Ghoshal IAC-MP I NBM 10 years ago

In our conversation, we outlined a better approach. Educational content. Light-lift podcasting. A brand narrative that reflects the real impact his team is making. No gimmicks. No pressure tactics. Just clear, human communication designed for the audience that matters most.

Educational storytelling is one of the few compliant marketing methods that builds real trust. When done well, it becomes an asset just as valuable as a strong investment strategy.

Advisors who want to scale, stand out, and attract premium clients cannot afford to rely on templated graphics and recycled corporate language. That approach is outdated. It does not match the weight of the work you are doing.

If you are building something meaningful and your content is not reflecting that value, it is time to rethink your visibility strategy.

Your story is not fluff. It is leverage. It

The longer I spent advising family offices, the more obvious one reality became: the model becomes harder to preserve as advisory businesses scale. Not because people lose standards or intent. Quite the opposite. Most firms are filled with exceptionally capable professionals trying to do the right thing for clients. But family offices are inherently difficult to systemise. Every family office is different. Different family dynamics. Different governance structures. Different sensitivities around liquidity, control, reputation, succession, and risk. The advice cannot become overly templated without losing part of what makes it valuable in the first place. That creates a genuine tension within large scale advisory models. As organisations grow, layers naturally emerge between senior experience and day to day client delivery. Over time, that distance matters. Fleming & Partners was built around the belief that highly personal, independent, institutional quality advice still matters deeply to family offices and endowments. The people leading the relationship are the people doing the work. Directly involved in the judgment, the portfolio discussions, the governance conversations, and the manager decisions. Not because it sounds differentiated. Because in our experience, it is the only way this segment can truly be advised properly. www.flemingandpartners.com

"If you’ve met one family office, you’ve met one family office." It is a phrase often repeated in our industry because it is true. Every family office has different governance dynamics, liquidity requirements, generational priorities, risk tolerances, and personal considerations sitting behind the portfolio. That individuality is precisely what makes high quality advisory difficult to scale. As advisory firms grow, standardisation becomes inevitable. Processes become centralised. Client relationships become layered. The challenge is no longer simply delivering good advice, but preserving genuinely independent and highly curated advice at scale. # Manual rebalancing slows wealth management teams

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

1mo

A small fixed income team at a regional bank. 700 accounts. Three systems that wouldn't talk to each other. And a spreadsheet for everything. Pull positions from one system. Run analytics in another. Flip to Excel to apply compliance restrictions — because every account had its own. Tax lot preferences, sector exclusions, client-directed holds, trust document constraints. No two accounts were built the same. In a banking environment, that's not a complaint. That's the job. Trust accounts are legally distinct. Wealth clients have IPS guidelines baked in by counsel. Institutional mandates have their own covenants. You can't batch what was never meant to be batched. So they didn't. They managed each one manually. And rebalancing 700 accounts — each its own snowflake — took 3 to 4 days. Not because the decisions were hard. Because the process made the complexity invisible until you were already buried in it. The bigger cost wasn't the time. It was the triage. Smaller accounts quietly received less attention — not by design, but because the workflow forced it. A $500K trust and a $5M institutional mandate can't get equal rigor when your team is building trade tickets one tab at a time. One platform changed that. Same two people. Same 700 accounts. Same regulatory environment. Rebalance time went from days to minutes — with every constraint, every customization, every compliance check still intact. The complexity didn't go away. It just stopped being manual. How much of your team's day is spent navigating the process instead of running the portfolio?

Pull data from one system, run analytics in another, toggle between spreadsheets for compliance, then manually build trade tickets one account at a time. I replaced our CRM with a git repo and an AI Agent

Agree & Join LinkedIn

photo credit of course to nano banana.

```
There's a quiet panic happening in SaaS right now. Valuations are compressing, usage-based pricing is replacing seats, and every founder in the space is asking the same question: what happens when AI agents can do the work my product charges humans to do?

Most of the conversation has centered on vertical SaaS and point solutions -the low-hanging fruit. The conventional wisdom is that systems of record are safe. Nobody is going to vibe-code a CRM. Nobody is going to prompt their way into an ERP. 

@akothari put it well in a recent post: "You're right that most companies will not vibe code their system of record." 

But then he makes the more interesting point -the question isn't whether companies replace Salesforce. It's whether your software can exist in the agentic ecosystem at all. Can agents read from it? Write to it? Operate autonomously within it? If not, your customers will eventually go somewhere they can.

Meanwhile, Karpathy coined "agentic engineering". The idea that the new default is you're not writing code directly, you're orchestrating agents and acting as oversight. He's talking about software development, but the pattern applies everywhere. The CLI is having a moment. Terminal-first, agent-operated workflows are becoming a legitimate way to build and run systems. Not just for coding for everything.

I didn't set out to prove any of this. I set out to solve a much smaller problem. I run a small venture fund, and the operational chaos of fund management: emails from founders, intro calls, pitch decks, WhatsApp threads, meeting notes, follow-ups was scattered across a CRM, Google Docs, email, and my head. The moment I wanted AI to help me reason over any of it, I hit a wall. The CRM had an API, sure, but getting an agent to authenticate, paginate, map fields, and handle rate limits for what amounts to "read this contact record" was absurd. So I tried something different.

I built the whole system on flat files and git. And it worked. And now I don't use the CRM at all.

---

## Why Markdown Is the Agent Interface

Here's something that doesn't get talked about enough: the best interface between a human and an AI agent isn't a GUI and it isn't an API. It's a plain text file.

content:
The Red Hot Chili Peppers Secret to Succession

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Inspiration, succession, and what John Frusciante knew that most new leaders don't.

Happy Monday.

I was sitting in the Delta lounge at O’Hare, waiting to fly home after an awesome week on the road.

NYC, Tampa, Atlanta, and Chicago. Perfect weather and even better presentations and talks.

Jud on the left. Chris Horvath of Taiko on the right. 🙂

Last Wednesday, we launched the first in a new webinar series with SignatureFD called How I AI—a behind-the-scenes look at how firms are actually adopting AI. Less theory, less hype, more practical application. Look for the next one soon.

Throughout the week, our team at Milemarker also shipped some incredible new features—things I've wanted to launch for a very long time. More on that shortly.

As I headed home, I wanted to share something I started writing on a flight out of LaGuardia last week. It’s about inspiration, succession, soul, and what happens when the songwriter is no longer at the helm.

Here's this week's Rising Tide.

1. The Red Hot Chili Peppers Secret to Succession
2. Podcast: How Advisors Can Beat the Biggest Retirement Risk 🎧
3. Milemarker Relay Launch
4. Milemarker On the Road ✈️

## The Red Hot Chili Peppers Secret to Succession

It's 7:45 pm on Monday. Wheels up from LGA, en route to Tampa. Caught a perfect glimpse of Brooklyn and lower Manhattan as the plane climbed.

Foo Fighters' new album, Your Favorite Toy, dropped Friday. The opener, Caught in the Echo, came out as a preview a few weeks back, and it's in my earbuds right now (that's right — I’m using the always-tangled corded version again). It has everything you could want in a rock song.

I recently watched the Red Hot Chili Peppers documentary — part behind-the-music, part tribute to Hillel Slovak, their original guitarist, who died in 1988.

So much of music is about inspiration. And inspiration isn't an equal sport. It usually comes down to one or two people who actually propel things forward. If they stall, retire, or pass, success free-falls. Few people talk about it openly, but the absence is palpable — everyone in the room trying to pin down what's missing, when the real answer is that the spark is gone.

So where am I going with this?

The vast majority of you reading this is the spark. You’re the songwriters. The lead vocalists. The hea

content:
Transforming Investor Onboarding: A Roadmap to Digitization

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

The asset management industry is at a turning point. As investors demand seamless, transparent, and efficient experiences, firms must evolve to meet these expectations. While digital transformation has reshaped many areas of financial services, investor onboarding has remained stubbornly complex, often relying on outdated, manual processes that create unnecessary friction.

Now, forward-thinking asset managers are embracing technology to modernize onboarding, not just for operational efficiency, but to revolutionize the investor experience. The question is no longer if firms will digitize, but how quickly they can adapt to changing investor expectations and industry trends.

Why Digitize Investor Onboarding?

Investor onboarding is a critical first impression. Traditionally, it has been a fragmented, paper-heavy process involving multiple stakeholders—legal, compliance, investor relations, and operations. This complexity creates delays, redundant document requests, and a lack of real-time visibility, frustrating investors who expect a frictionless experience.

At a time when technology is enabling instant access to information and seamless digital interactions across industries, investors increasingly expect the same from their asset managers. Long processing times, manual forms, and lack of status transparency are no longer acceptable.

The cost of inefficiency is high—not just in operational expenses but also in potential lost opportunities. Delays in onboarding can slow down capital deployment, disrupt fund closings, and impact investor trust. A digital-first approach ensures that investors receive the speed, transparency, and efficiency they now demand.

The Future of Investor Onboarding: A Technology-Enabled Experience

A digital onboarding solution integrates technology across people, processes, and tools. The goal is to automate manual tasks, centralize data, and improve collaboration between teams, ensuring that each investor receives a smooth, consistent experience.

content:
AI Efficiency vs Financial Advisor Talent Pipeline | Michael Kitces posted on the topic | LinkedIn

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Chief Financial Planning Nerd

1w

Now, we seem to largely be past the, "AI is going to replace all the financial advisors." I feel like we've shifted from, "AI is the threat" to now, "No, no, no, AI is going to solve the talent shortage by making us hyper-efficient so we can all handle way more clients." https://kitc.es/4ds48Uj But the challenge I have to it is, I'm supposed to be getting more efficient because by eliminating all these back and middle office tasks to free up the time and have better margins and bring down the costs, all the ideally good things.... I gutted all the entry-level jobs that train everybody to eventually get to the end state job where you're an experienced advisor. 20 years from now or 10 years from now go, "Wow, our advisors seem really experienced and they're great, but they're retiring soon. And we have no young people in this firm and no young people in the talent pipeline. And we don't even know how to develop talent because we spent the past ten years gutting every entry-level position in our firm." More in this episode of Kitces & Carl (Carl Richards) on YouTube, Apple, Spotify.🎧#podcast#practicemanagement#financialadvisorpodcast

Transcript Transcript

##### Transcript

I can tell a story that. The most successful advisory firm 20 years from now will be the one that completely eschews all that AI stuff. Should we optimize every little thing for efficiency? Do those models work when you're talking about emotionally impactful presence work? You know, like, let alone the financial planning you're doing behind the scenes by eliminating all these back and middle office tasks to free up the time and have better margins and bring down the costs. Ideally, good thing, good thing. Yeah, they gutted all the entry level jobs that train everybody to eventually get to the end state job where you're an experienced advisor. Are we trying to get a productivity boom from AI to solve our talent shortage that will then drastically exacerbate our talent shortage because we got our own talent pipelines?

Paradino AI co-founder and CEO | AI-first platform for Financial Advisers | Ex Adviser

1w

Working as a paraplanner to become an adviser was the most inefficient way for me to have been trained for that

AI, Alternatives and Governance: How I Read the 2026 JP Morgan Global Family Office Report

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Here is how I see it...

The JP Morgan 2026 Global Family Office Report landed this week. 333 single family offices across 30 countries. $518 billion in collective net worth surveyed.

And if you read it carefully, not just the charts but the fault lines between the data, it tells a story that goes well beyond portfolio allocation.

Let me give you my read. First order. Second order. And a few third order implications that I think most advisors are not yet pricing in.

---

FIRST ORDER: What the numbers say on the surface

The headline allocations are striking but not surprising. Public equities at 38.4% and private investments at 30.8% together command over two thirds of portfolios.

Private equity leads planned increases, with 37% of family offices intending to raise exposure in the next 12 to 18 months.

But two gaps stand out as commercially and legally significant.

First, the AI allocation paradox. 65% of family offices say AI is a priority investment theme, yet 57% have zero exposure to growth equity or venture capital and 79% have zero allocation to infrastructure, the physical backbone of AI in the form of power, data centres and logistics. This is not sophisticated investing. It is narrative without deployment.

Second, the gold and crypto contradiction. Geopolitics is the number one cited risk by 20% of family offices globally. Yet 72% hold zero gold and 89% hold zero crypto. These families are talking risk hedging without executing it.

The immediate commercial and legal implication is clear. Advisors and fund managers who can credibly bridge the AI intent to allocation gap through GP led structures, co investment rights in infrastructure platforms or access to late stage growth equity will be well placed to capture mandates.

The demand signal is clear. The structuring and product architecture have not yet caught up.

---

SECOND ORDER: What the structural dynamics tell us

If we look beyond the allocation charts, a more consequential story appears.

First, the governance deficit is a legal time bomb. 86% of family offices globally do not have clear succession plans for key decision makers. 41% of business owning families identify internal conflict as a top three risk, nearly double their non business owning peers.
title: AI, Alternatives and Governance: How I Read the 2026 JP Morgan Global Family Office Report

At Some Point, Wealth Needs Structure: A Founder's Framework for When to Build a Single Family Office

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

I've sat across the table from a particular kind of founder more times than I can count.

Late forties, early fifties. Built the business from nothing. Still signs most things himself. His accountant has been with him for fifteen years. His children are starting to ask questions about the business. His wife is starting to ask different ones.

And somewhere in the conversation, almost always, he says:

"I'm managing everything myself. Why do I need a Single Family Office Single Family Office?"

For years, my honest answer was: maybe you don't yet.

But I've watched enough founders cross the line from managing wealth to being managed by it that I've developed a framework for when the answer changes.

The misconception

Most founders think a Single Family Office is about managing investments. It isn't. Banks do that. Asset managers do that. You don't need an SFO for that.

An SFO exists to be the single point of coordination across everything your wealth touches — tax, legal entities, succession, philanthropy, lifestyle assets, governance, the education of the next generation, and the administrative machinery that holds it all together.

When all of that sits in one founder's head, the founder is the family office. Which works — until it doesn't.

The four signals

When two are present, it's time to start structuring. When three are present, you're already late.

1. Your wealth has outgrown your bandwidth. There's a moment when wealth stops being something you manage in the margins of running your business and becomes its own full-time job. You miss deadlines. You forget which entity owns what. You make decisions slower because you're tired, not because they're hard.

2. Your structure has outgrown your memory. How many legal entities do you have, across how many jurisdictions? Who are the directors? Which one owns the holiday home, which the operating business, which the investments? If you can't answer in under sixty seconds, the structure has outgrown your memory. The cost of forgetting in this domain isn't a missed appointment — it's a missed filing, an expired residency, an exposure no one was watching.

3. Your family has questions you don't have time to answer. Children grow up. Spouses get involved. The family be
title: At Some Point, Wealth Needs Structure: A Founder's Framework for When to Build a Single Family Office

Family Offices: Reporting Obligations and Transparency

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Family offices must navigate a complex landscape of reporting obligations and transparency requirements. This sub-section is dedicated to offering a comprehensive insight into understanding these responsibilities and ensuring compliance while maintaining the confidentiality and trust of the clients.

---

### 1. Understanding Reporting Obligations:

Different jurisdictions may have various reporting requirements, including tax reporting, anti-money laundering (AML) reports, disclosure of significant holdings, and more. Familiarity with both international standards and local laws is a critical first step in fulfilling these obligations.

---

### 2. Tax Reporting:

Family offices must comply with tax reporting regulations in the jurisdictions where they operate and where their clients are domiciled. This includes income tax, capital gains tax, gift tax, and other relevant taxes. Engaging with tax professionals who are well-versed in international and local tax laws can help ensure compliance.

---

3. AML Reporting:

Compliance with AML regulations involves not just understanding the source of funds but also reporting suspicious activities to the relevant authorities. Establishing robust AML procedures and ensuring that all staff members are aware of their responsibilities is essential.

---

### 4. Financial Statements and Audits:

Depending on the jurisdiction and the structure of the family office, there may be requirements to prepare and submit financial statements, possibly audited by an independent third party. This necessitates maintaining accurate and transparent financial records and engaging qualified auditors when required.

---

### 5. Regulatory Reporting for Investments:

Investment activities may trigger additional reporting obligations, such as disclosure of substantial shareholdings or compliance with specific investment regulations. Understanding these obligations and working closely with legal and investment professionals can facilitate compliance.

---

### 6. Transparency and Client Communication:

Transparency is not just a regulatory requirement but also a foundation for trust with clients. Clearly communicating with clients about the family office's activities, performance, and decisions can enhance relationships while maintaining compliance

### The moment I ran the check on myself

A few weeks ago, I pointed four AI models at my own business to audit the messaging and see whether they would recommend Polything to a sceptical £2–7m UK B2B founder Four independent views of my public account and Polything’s public surfaces, and scored the way a sceptical £2–7m UK B2B founder would score them.

The results were not comfortable reading.

Between my homepage, about page, LinkedIn headline, and an /ai page, and external messaging the audits triangulated five different ICPs sitting next to each other: D2C consumer brands on one surface, “scale-ups to £10m” on another, “£1–10m B2C and SaaS” on LinkedIn, “£2–7m UK B2B” on /ai, and neurodivergent founder coaching off to the side. What I found was that each page was a slightly different take on who I served, written accurately at the time but now lying forgotten.

The outputs were hard to take as a strategist. Claude said, “Don’t take the call” and ChatGPT said that my “positioning is tighter than the proof.” (Polything cross-audit, 20 April 2026).

At that moment, I felt the reaction that every founder feels. Hire a copywriter, commission new content, and spend more on paid. Do the thing that looks like marketing.

But I didn’t. The gap those four audits triangulated was not a copywriting problem. It was simply a clarity gap created by years of content and messaging, and no amount of spend could have papered over it. The actual work was creating a single sentence that the whole public messaging footprint could align around.

My recent Polything newsletter on trendslop was about the content founders consume instead of doing the strategic work. This one is about the implications of skipping that work and paying someone else to just paper over it. Altair Partners describes the move as an outsourcing strategy before it has been written down (Altair, 2025). It often appears to be a marketing problem, but almost never is.

The Day Marcus Almost Fired His Marketing Agency (And What He Learned Instead)

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Marcus had done everything right.

He hired a digital marketing agency. He approved the strategy deck. He paid the monthly retainer without complaint. He posted on LinkedIn three times a week because someone told him consistency was the key.

Eighteen months later, he had a website that looked polished, a social media feed that stayed active, and a pipeline that had not grown in a year.

He was not failing. He was busy. And busy had started to feel like the same thing as stuck.

Marcus runs a financial advisory firm in the Dallas area. Solid reputation. Good clients. A team that genuinely cares about the people they serve. The kind of business that should be growing faster than it is. He called me after being referred by a mutual contact, frustrated and skeptical, which I respected.

The first thing he said was, "I don't think I need more marketing. I think I need to understand why what I'm already doing isn't working."

He was right. He just did not know how right he was.

---

The Activity Trap

Most professional services firms are not suffering from a lack of marketing. They are suffering from a lack of strategy.

There is a difference. A real one.

Activity is posting content, running ads, updating the website, sending newsletters. Strategy is knowing which clients you actually want, why they should choose you over the firm down the street, and what specific actions will reach them in a way that produces a conversation.

Marcus had activity. He did not have strategy.

His agency was doing exactly what agencies are built to do. They were executing. Producing. Delivering on the agreed scope of work. The problem was not that they were bad at their jobs. The problem was that no one was doing the senior-level thinking that has to happen before any of that execution means anything.

That is not a criticism of agencies. It is an honest description of what agencies are designed to provide and what they are not.

---

What Was Actually Missing

When I sat down with Marcus, the first thing I asked was not about his marketing. I asked about his best clients.

What did they have in common? How did they find him? What was going on in their business when they decided to look for an advisor? What did they say when they referred someone?

The Common Marketing Struggles Wealth Managers Face in 2025 – And How to Fix Them

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Alexis Mitchell - wealthiemarketing.com

In 2025, wealth managers are navigating a rapidly changing marketing landscape. Many are finding that traditional marketing practices just don’t deliver the results they once did. Whether it’s print ads, cold calling, or relying solely on referrals, wealth managers are struggling to adapt to a more digital-first, compliance-driven environment. So, what’s causing these struggles, and how can wealth managers fix them?

### Outdated Practices: Why They Don’t Work Anymore

1. Print Advertising Once the go-to for reaching high-net-worth individuals, print advertising has lost its impact. Research shows that digital marketing is now the preferred channel for wealth management clients. According to a recent study by EY, 62% of affluent investors say they prefer engaging with financial firms online. While print ads may still have a place in highly localized markets, their ROI is shrinking rapidly in a world where people expect instant, online interactions.
2. Cold Calling Cold calling has been a staple for decades, but in 2025, it’s simply ineffective. Consumers, especially younger generations, are inundated with unsolicited outreach, making them more resistant to traditional sales tactics. A study from Salesforce reveals that 89% of buyers want to engage in a consultative sales approach, not cold calls. Wealth managers need to embrace digital communication channels that allow them to nurture leads through value-based content and targeted outreach.
3. Over-reliance on Referrals Referrals, though still important, can’t be the sole driver of growth. The market is more competitive than ever, and relying exclusively on referrals can leave firms vulnerable to stagnation. While 88% of high-net-worth investors still trust referrals, they are also looking for firms that demonstrate expertise online. Failing to build a strong digital presence could mean missing out on new business.

The following article is an excerpt section from our free guide to the "Hyper Boutique RIA Strategy". You can download the free guide, which includes a detailed implementation playbook, here. 

 

## Introduction 

 

The RIA space is scratching heads as to why organic growth rates have dipped into 3% territory (read: I haven't heard any good explanations yet, so here goes!). 

 

Meanwhile, SaaS (Software as a Service) enjoys 23% average growth rates. And SaaS aimed at being a substitute for a real advisor is cleaning house. For example, Betterment (a SaaS platform and robo-substitute for an RIA/IAR) boasts a 69% growth rate. 

 

Anecdotal evidence that demand for financial advice is still high. 

 

Fig. 1: Google Trends analysis shows a steady uptick on the search term "financial advisor", since 2010. 

 

Betterment is a great case study. They are currently peeling NextGen away from RIA not because of any one channel or tactic. But because they have applied "money brain" to their marketing. 

 

Here's what "money brain" on digital marketing means (it's so simple, RIAs should be kicking themselves that they've been SO SLOW to adopt their own advice): 

 

Betterment has a diversified digital marketing portfolio. They have assets and activities that meet users where they are at, speaking to prospects at all stages of the modern client journey. They have great "earned media" (e.g. social and podcast), but also fantastic "owned media" (e.g. landing pages and nurture sequences) and "paid media" (e.g. Pay-per-click Ads and remarketing tech). 

 

Betterment is leveraging tagging, tracking, and remarketing, to serve up sweetened offers in social media feeds, to folks who've visited their website and landing pages, but have yet to convert. They are staying top of mind. They have landing pages that are designed to convert at high rates, because they have proven CRO (conversion rate optimization) design principles applied. And they have real, tangible, offers to the market (see images below).

Why YouTube Views Don't Equal New Clients for Advisors | Kendra Wright posted on the topic | LinkedIn

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

# Why YouTube Views Don't Equal New Clients for Advisors

Talented advisor but bad at marketing? I fix that. | Owner: Rebel Media Agency

1mo

3 years posting on Youtube. 10,000-20,000+ views per video. Only 1 new client after all this effort. 🤯 Why? This week, I had a conversation with an advisor that has a successful practice (7 million+) but nearly 100% of all new clients are referrals. From the outside looking in, this firm looks like it is CRUSHING it on YouTube. ✅ Great views (some as high as 300K+) ✅ Stellar production (looks sharp) ✅ Deep expertise (20+ years as advisor) So what’s the issue with their underperforming YouTube funnel? After a quick review, this is what I found: 1️⃣ Paid ads were inflating the traffic numbers On the front end, the numbers fantastic! On the backend, this firm was spending thousands (!!!) per month on paid ads for YouTube. This isn’t wrong but the views weren’t telling the full story. ❌ What advisors can learn: If you get significant views/reach on content but no clients overtime, something is broken in your marketing funnel. 2️⃣ Mismatched content format All the videos were in a podcast format. I’ve seen multiple YouTube channels driving fantastic lead flow. Guess what none of them do? Publish a podcast. I’m not a fan of repurposing podcasts to YouTube. This is expensive to do and in my experience typically drives low to no results for firms. I'm sure there are outliers here but they are exactly that - not typical. ❌ What advisors can learn: Podcasts and YouTube channels are different beasts with radically different growth mechanics and behavior. You aren't growing your podcast if you publish to YouTube, you are growing an entirely new channel. 3️⃣ Lack of clear calls to action to the bottom of the funnel There was no clear calls to action that moved people into the bottom of the funnel (the sales process). You don’t need to hit people over the head but you do need to regularly and repeatedly invite them to the next step. ❌ What advisors can learn: On all platforms you need to make it clear: “I’m a professional advisor and can help you with this exact topic. Click the link the description to book a call.” Short, non-pushy. Do it a few minutes into the video (not the end). - - - -

The Alternative Investments Opportunity: Traditional Asset Managers at a Strategic Crossroads

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

In my discussions with traditional asset managers, alternative investments are the hottest topic, bar none. Nothing else is even close. The landscape of asset management is rapidly evolving, with alternative investments emerging as a critical frontier. This shift is reshaping strategies for both traditional asset managers and private market firms, as they vie for the lucrative high-net-worth (HNW) investor segment. This article explores the current state of play, the strategies being employed, and the challenges faced in this dynamic market.

For this discussion, I define alternative investments as non-traditional asset classes and strategies delivered in product structures that are not open-end mutual funds, ETFs, or other daily-liquid or exchange-traded vehicles. This encompasses a spectrum from interval funds to institutional-style drawdown funds, and everything in between. The target market for this discussion is HNW investors who, heretofore, have not had many compelling alternative investments choices.

This product space is shaping up to be a competition between two factions. One side includes traditional asset managers seasoned in serving the mass market and HNW investors with conventional offerings. The other side includes private markets firms, experts in serving institutions and family offices that make multi-million-dollar commitments to a single fund. Both are now maneuvering to capture the under-served and lucrative middle ground of the HNW market.

Discussions with traditional asset managers are wide-ranging, but they always touch on the key points of whether, when, and how to launch and grow an alternative investments business line. Despite broad consensus on the topic's importance and the need to define a strategy, agreement stops there as there is a wide disparity of opinions and strategies. The approaches traditional asset managers are taking with alternative investments business space can be segmented into three distinct camps.

Camp 1: Aggressive Entrants. The first camp’s strategy is primarily inorganic. These players are moving aggressively forward with the view that the traditional markets are in long-term revenue decline, and a transformational move into alternative investments is necessary and pruden
title: The Alternative Investments Opportunity: Traditional Asset Managers at a Strategic Crossroads

Source: The Decent Millionaire

### And how bolder allocation models will fuel the growth of advisory AUM, retail portfolios and the economy at large

I recently penned an article underscoring why 2025 will inaugurate humanity’s alternative era. In the piece, I discussed how the alternative fraction of every industry known to man – particularly finance, media and health – will rise and reign.

Given the acceleration of innovation in the financial services sector, it should come as no surprise that the investment advisory business is one that will be most impacted by this alternative uprising.

Not only is the alternative asset universe evolving and proliferating, but the vehicles being used to hold and custody these next-generation alternative assets are advancing at unprecedented rates.

These financial product modifications will reimagine retirement planning and transform portfolio construction – particularly for retail clientele - who now, more than ever, require greater portfolio diversification and an even vaster array of alternative assets.

Although the majority of financial advisors have now come to view alternative assets as a core component of wealth management, 2025 will be the year that their allocation models start to become bolder, more creative, and quite frankly, far more effective in generating better risk-adjusted returns.

Below are five key reasons why all financial advisors will be recommending next-generation alternative assets and how these changes in their allocation models will fuel the growth of advisory AUM, retail portfolios and the economy at large.

1. Alternative Assets are vital to enhancing Sharpe Ratios

The Sharpe Ratio is a commonly used metric that analyzes risk-adjusted returns. Essentially, the higher the Sharpe Ratio, the better the risk-adjusted return. And diversification has long been proven to enhance the Sharpe Ratio of a portfolio.

Unfortunately, portfolios comprised solely of traditional assets like stocks, bonds and mutual funds are rapidly becoming less and less diversified over time. The reason is simple. These traditional assets are all derivatives of the same small diminishing pool of publicly-traded companies.

How to Avoid SEC Fines in 2024

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

The SEC dished out fines to 26 advisory firms for failure to maintain current and accurate records of electronic communications, accumulating $392.75 million and leading firms to enhance their compliance procedures.

If we have said it once, we have said it a thousand times: the SEC is continuing to crack down on text messaging archiving and reviews, and they don’t seem to be slowing down anytime soon.

Debrief of current SEC fines

The SEC’s recent investigation uncovered ‘pervasive and longstanding use of unapproved communication methods, known as off-channel communications’ from personnel at all levels of authority and include broker-dealers, investment advisers, and dually-registered broker-dealers and investment advisers; as well as firm supervisors and senior managers.

Each of the 26 firms were charged with violating certain recordkeeping provisions of the Securities Exchange Act, the Investment Advisers Act, or both. In addition to these regulatory fines, the firms were charged with failing to reasonably supervise their personnel to prevent such violations.

Record keeping and supervisory requirements are essential to investor protection and preserving well-functioning markets; failure to comply deprives the SEC of critical information during its investigations and leads to increased fines in the long run.

It should be noted that of the 26 firms affected, three – Truist Securities, Cetera Advisor Networks, and Hilltop Securities – self-reported their violations and significantly decreased their penalties.

Gurbir S. Grewal, Director of the SEC’s Division of Enforcement, emphasized the importance of compliance with recordkeeping regulations, highlighting the benefits of proactive cooperation as demonstrated by the self-reporting firms.

A simple solution to record-keeping for the SEC

Our Simple solutions include: The Compliance Portal, Email and SMS Archive, Trade Monitor, and The Compliance Calendar.

In late March 2022, the SEC Division of Examinations (“Exams Division”) set forth strategic priorities for the year to restore “trust necessary for our markets to thrive," during a “time of heightened market volatility.” According to leadership, last year’s emphasis was on “emerging issues, such as crypto-assets and expanding information security threats, as well as core compliance gaps affecting retail investors.” (See Bates 2022 exam priorities summary and chart.)

Ten months later, on February 7, 2023, the Exams Division announced a new focus, reflecting a shift in priorities for 2023 based on the need to adapt to “growing markets, evolving technologies, and new forms of risk.” The emphasis in this year’s SEC priorities announcement remains on protecting retail investors. However, the new priorities reflect the agency’s latest rulemakings, compliance expectations around earlier rulemakings and further adjustments toward “a risk-based approach to examination selection that balances our resources across a diverse registrant base.” In the report, the Exams Division is emphasizing compliance with the new marketing rule, new investment company regulations on derivatives and fair valuation, expectations around Regulation Best Interest (“Reg BI”), and registered investment advisers’ duties as to private funds.

While there is overlap concerning subject matter between last year and this year (see Bates annual priorities comparison chart below), the priority shift toward ensuring compliance with the new rules has important implications for all regulated market participants. Here is our summary of the announced priorities for 2023.

## SEC Leadership Messages

In the report, the SEC Exams Division leadership team reported on their efforts over the past year to: (i) respond to continued “market volatility, cyber-events, and market disruptions caused by recent bankruptcies and financial distress among crypto asset market participants;” (ii) promote compliance through risk alerts, exam deficiency communications and the instant priorities report; (iii) communicate about focused exams and enforcement sweep initiatives; (iv) engage in national and regional office proactive outreach; and (v) convey useful observations and information to the
title: SEC 2023 Exam Priorities and Comparison Chart: New Marketing Rule, Reg BI, and Private Funds

The Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) has introduced new Anti-Money Laundering (AML) and Countering the Financing of Terrorism (CFT) regulations for Registered Investment Advisers (RIAs). These new rules will take effect on January 1, 2026, bringing significant changes to the compliance landscape for investment advisers. 

 

### What RIAs Need to Know About FinCEN’s New AML Rule 

 

With the new rule, RIAs are officially classified as “financial institutions” under the Bank Secrecy Act (BSA). This classification means RIAs must comply with stringent AML/CFT standards designed to protect the financial system's transparency and integrity. 

 

To meet these requirements, RIAs must implement a risk-based AML/CFT program tailored to their business risks and clientele. The program must include policies, procedures, and internal controls that help detect and report suspicious activity. Additionally, it must be approved by the firm’s board of directors or equivalent authority. 

 

### Independent Testing and Employee Training for AML Compliance 

 

RIAs are also required to conduct independent testing of their AML/CFT program. This can be done by internal personnel or third-party providers, ensuring the program stays effective and compliant. Regular reviews should be scheduled throughout the year to adapt to changing regulatory landscapes. 

 

Each RIA must assign a responsible party to oversee the program’s implementation and handle any issues that may arise. Employee training is also crucial, as it helps staff understand the risks associated with money laundering and terrorism financing, ensuring they comply with AML rules. 

 

### Reporting Suspicious and High-Value Transactions 

 

FinCEN’s new rule requires RIAs to file Suspicious Activity Reports (SARs) for transactions of $5,000 or more, aligning their responsibilities with those of other financial institutions. Additionally, RIAs must file Currency Transaction Reports (CTRs) for transactions over $10,000 in currency, replacing the previous Form 8300 requirement. 

 

### How RIAs Can Prepare for FinCEN’s New AML Rule

## Not understanding or misinterpreting regulations 

 

Whether dealing with conflicts of interest, advertising, record-keeping, or cybersecurity, RIAs operate in a world of constantly changing regulations. Simply keeping up with the latest changes can be a challenge, which is why a well-organized compliance department is essential. 

 

Some of the more common issues in this area include:- 

 

### Lack of familiarity with SEC regulations 

 

A key role of the compliance department is communicating regulations to the broader workforce. First, they must understand the changes, create the relevant procedures and handbooks, and ensure the information is presented in a simple-to-understand format. 

 

For example, the SEC Marketing Rule (Rule 206(4)-1) consolidates previous regulations covering advertising and cash solicitation rules, bringing them into a single framework. It clearly defines:- 

 

- Types of advertising 
- The use of testimonials and endorsements 
- Acceptable performance statistics 
- Record-keeping requirements 

 

 

Historically, areas of this regulation have been open to interpretation, but this is not true today. 

 

### Misunderstanding fiduciary duty requirements 

 

While many of the elements of fiduciary duty are obvious and straightforward, a lack of attention to detail has seen some firms receiving financial penalties. There are numerous duties, such as:- 

 

- Due care and attention 
- Avoiding conflicts-of-interest 
- Full disclosure 
- Competency 
- Safeguarding client assets 
- Putting client interests first 
- Confidentiality
